name: Peter Appleyard
abstract: Peter Appleyard,  (26 August 1928 – 17 July 2013) was a British–Canadian jazz vibraphonist, percussionist, and composer. He spent most of his life in the city of Toronto, where for many years he was a popular performer in nightclubs and hotels such as The Park Plaza, Stop 33, The 54th, The Chelsea Inn, and The Montreal Bistro.

quotes.text: Well sometimes people were taken to task. I never really saw him lose his temper, but Hank Jones, who is my favorite pianist, I mean he was so beautiful, incredible player, and Dick feels the same way, on that record, you know, he’s on that record that I sent you. He was booked to be with us at some special function for the government and didn’t show up. He told Benny that he’d been held up at the George Washington Bridge and couldn’t make it. Benny called the New York Port Authority and found out there was no hold up on the bridge that day. So the next time he came to the states Benny took him to task and they started a fight on the stage. Not physically, but Hank, Hank was very genteel and very proper, and he was cursing at Benny. So he walked off the stage. Benny never used him again. Then on three occasions I booked Hank Jones and he didn’t show up. The record I made over in Barbados was done with Bucky, Major Holly, Butch Miles, Rick Wilkins on tenor, and myself, without the piano. He was booked to play in Barbados. They even rented a German Steinway for him. And I called him two nights before we were to go on, because he was at Carnegie Hall with Ella. I said, “Hank, we’ve got a room at the airport on the night before the flight. We’d like you to be there with us so we can all leave early in the morning.” “Oh, I’ll be there,” he said, “what kind of shoes do you want me to wear?” I said, “I don’t care if you come in your bare feet but be there.” “Oh, I’ll be there.” He didn’t show up. So when we got to Barbados I had to make excuses for him. So I called him back again and I said, “What happened?” He said, “I forgot my passport.” So then he said, “Well I’ll be on the plane tomorrow.” So we drove all the way across the island to pick him up, he didn’t show up. So I called him again, I said, “Hank, what are you doing?” “Oh,” he said, “I didn’t like the attitude of the clerk.”
quotes.caption: Hank Jones missed Barbados gig after playing with Ella at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Peter Appleyard interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, October 4, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Warren Vaché
about_quotes.evidence: I did my first overseas trip in 1974 with Benny and John Bunch and Bucky Pizzarelli, Barrett Deems, and I think Urbie was on that one, Peter Appleyard on vibes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Warren Vaché interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 23, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Bennett
about_quotes.evidence: I was on the road with Bucky for a little bit too, and Peter Appleyard... I got to spent a lot of times talking with Bucky and Peter Appleyard
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Bennett interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 16, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Gene Bertoncini
about_quotes.evidence: And Peter Appleyard, and a lady bass player, Nicki Parrott, and Joe Ascione and Bucky Pizzarelli. And it was for label called Arbors Records.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gene Bertoncini interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: Peter Appleyard played vibes, and one night Lionel came in and sat in with us.
